editionStmt.p: This new digital translation of John of Ephesus’s Ecclesiastical History, Part 3 has been created within the context of the DFG Project "Commentary on John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History." The current version (Book 1) should be considered preliminary, as it will be updated throughout the duration of the project. Philip Michael Forness translated the text from the new edition of the Ecclesiastical History produced within the context of the project. The translation benefitted enormously from the help of Silvio Roggo, Hartmut Leppin, and Nikolai Kiel who offered feedback, suggestions, and corrections as the commentary was produced. Persons, places, and bible passages have been tagged by Philip Michael Forness, Silvio Roggo, and the project's student assistants (Katharina Koch, Marie Skubowius, and Eliano Veronesi).

cRefPattern.p: For references to the Peshitta Old and New Testaments, PES is used as the abbreviation.
Noted are references to a biblical book, chapter, verse, sc. Peshitta:Gn:1:1; also possible: Peshitta:Gn:1:1-3 for a sequence of verses or LXX:Gn:1:1.3 for two verses not following each other.

The following abbreviations are used: Gn, Ex, Lv, Num, Dt, Jos, Judg, Rt, 1Sa, 2Sa, 1Ko, 2Ko, 1Chr, 2Chr, 3Esr, Esr, Est, Jdt, Tob, 1Mak, 2Mak, 3Mak, 4Mak, Ps, Oden, Prov, Eccl, Song, Job, Wis, Sir, PsSal, Hos, Am, Mi, Joel, Ob, Jon, Nah, Hab, Zeph, Hag, Sach, Mal, Is, Jr, Bar, Lam, EpistJer, Hes, Sus, Dn, Bel; Mt, Mk, Lk, Jn, Act, Rom, 1Cor, 2Cor, Gal, Eph, Phil, Col, 1Th, 2Th, 1Tim, 2Tim, Tt, Phm, Heb, Jak, 1P, 2P, 1Jn, 2Jn, 3Jn, Jud, Rev.
Noted are references to a biblical book and chapter, sc. Peshitta:Gn:1; also possible: Peshitta:Gn:1-3 for a sequence of chapters or Peshitta:Gn:1.3 for two chapters not following each other.
Noted are references to a biblical book, sc. Peshitta:Gn.

interpretation.p: This file is encoded according to the guidelines of the Patristic Text Archive (PTA). See https://pta.bbaw.de/project/encoding-guidelines and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3737666.
Biblical quotations are annotated and referenced. References follow the scheme declared above.

Persons are annotated and referenced with IDs according to the TIPNR - Tyndale Individualised Proper Names with all References list for biblical persons and IDs of PTA persons index or of Syriaca.org for all other persons.
Groups of persons and organisations are not annotated.
Places are annotated and referenced with Pleiades Gazetteer or Syriaca.org IDs.
Words are not tagged and not analyzed.

seg.supplied: The Ecclesiastical History of Holy Mar John, the Bishop of the City of Ephesus: Part 3

div.p:
the anger of God has been awakened. That is, all these schisms, quarrels, divisions, and along with these the frequent, bitter persecutions and the harsh imprisonments and bonds which we see – all of these are among the final birth pangs.
For, indeed, already at that time our Savior also proclaimed and said:
Repent! The kingdom of heaven has drawn near to you!
He also brought forth lucid evidence, saying clearly:
When you have seen all these things coming to pass, know that it has arrived at the door.
Moreover, about these things which are happening now among us, he also taught and made known to his disciples beforehand – not only to the great company of the apostles but also to all the disciples after them and until today – speaking in this way:
Beware of people, for they will hand you over to the courts and flog you in their synagogues. They will lead you before governors and kings for my sake as a testimony for them and for the nations. But when they hand you over, do not worry how or what you will say, for what you will say will be given to you at that hour. For it is not you speaking, but the spirit of our Father will be speaking in you. A brother will hand over his brother to death, and a father his child. Children will rise against their parents and kill them. You will be hated by everyone for my name’s sake.
These are the things that we now see clearly, which have come even now in this time to all the members of the church and have been fulfilled. We are enduring these attacks and wickedness not from outsiders and pagans but even from those who are counted as Christians but in fact freely carry out pagan acts against their fellow Christians without the bridles of the fear of God, without mercy, in a cruel and savage manner, without any hindrance. So far concerning all these things. In the original composition, we previously, in two parts, with much effort, over an extended period of time told the stories of the church insofar as we were able within twelve volumes in which all the different chapters are indicated. We began from the time of Julius Caesar and the rest of the early Roman emperors and then sequentially assembled and set in order the stories of the church until the sixth year of the reign of Justin II, the son of Justinian’s sister . As for the same emperor Justin’s diligence and concern for the unity of the church from the beginning of his reign until his sixth year, we also carefully and diligently testified to this while clinging to the truth in the accounts that we previously related. But now because we see that a harsh storm has suddenly, vehemently, and intensely swelled up, thundering on account of our sins, with the intensity of its frequent and numerous waves, against the firm ship of the whole church of the orthodox – it is for this reason that we also have been moved to commit the records, even if only a few, to writing, though time on all sides would hold us back and impede us from great length and prevent us from relating insofar as possible even briefly the narrative of the ecclesiastical stories, which even now are taking place once again. Although we are setting down just a few of them from many in the records, we will bring them to the knowledge of those after us through this third composition so that they might also be clearly and precisely known to all posterity, even if it has not escaped me that the times of the world have already been drawing to a close and passing away. Even if this should only happen in the short time that remains for this turbulent world, we have committed them to writing in brief records for the knowledge of posterity.

head.title: Chapter 4: From the books of the prophets on the sign of tribulation that happened in the church of God at this time.

div.p:
So now, at the beginning of the stories in this book, the groans of the suffering prophet Jeremiah’s Lamentations
would be suitable, which he at that time voiced and cried out in regard to Jerusalem and its people which had fallen by the sword. As a result, we might also lament and cry out in the same way regarding those things which have happened at this time in the church of God because of our iniquities and many sins.
So also would Isaiah, illustrious among the prophets, shout at the top of his lungs without holding back and raise his voice like a trumpet upon seeing the harsh chastisement that has come upon the church of God. He would also add and recount, saying:
The whole head in pain, the whole heart in sadness, from the sole of the foot to the brain, there is no place that is sound in it, but rather bruises, sores, and swollen wounds that cannot be bound up, bandaged, nor soothed with oil.
Then, he would recount with suffering and say:
The earth lamented, it dwelt in mourning, the inhabited world lamented, dwelt, and mourned. The height
Or: “the pride.”
of the earth lamented. The earth has become like its inhabitants.
Therefore, it would be fitting for the holy ones such as these to suffer with us with groaning regarding the great anger which on account of our sins has been sent upon the church of God all of a sudden, just as at that time it was sent upon Jerusalem. In regard to the fear-inducing terrors that suddenly roared, shaking and disturbing all the faithful people who had been living in and enjoying peace and tranquility for an extended period of time in the imperial city and in all its suburbs until the sixth year of the reign of the victorious Justin , and the sudden snare of anger that sprung on all the communities of the church of the orthodox, casting them down and scattering them to each of the winds – I have little time to write and tell about these at length.

head.title: Chapter 5: About the bitter suffering of the sudden flight of all the communities of the church of the faithful in the imperial city.

div.p:
For an extended period of time of more than forty years all the communities of the whole church of the orthodox had lived in tranquility and harmony in this great imperial city and in all its suburbs. In great freedom of assembly, openly, in great numbers, and without fear, all the communities of the faithful would gather together in every place and would carry out all the ecclesiastical mysteries and liturgies. Then suddenly during the holy days of the fast of forty days, on Saturday when the Sunday of Hosannas was beginning to dawn,
at the insistence and wicked vehemence of the one who was in charge of the church in the imperial city, namely, John
Marginal note by a later hand: “Woe to him!”
from the village of Sirmin in Syria, who repeatedly slandered the whole side of the orthodox, the victorious Justin was awakened with severe anger. With a threat of great rage, he ordered all the gathering places of all the faithful to be seized, all the altars in all of them to be torn out, all the priests and their bishops to be apprehended and cast into prison, and all those gathered in them to be expelled and driven out so that they might not gather together again. There were other such things that were ordered and decreed out of great anger. While previously up to that point […] harmoniously and quietly in the whole city

“[…] why do you sit as a Christian alongside those who sent me to you and judge in a pagan way the servants of God? You are not among the living, unless you immediately set me, a weak old man, on fire and eat me roasted.” As soon as the prefect heard these things and was shaken by the weight of his old age, he issued an order and he was brought out of his presence and taken to the bishop. Out of his madness,
“Madness” is crossed out in the manuscript with a sign that points towards a marginal reading that has “his rage.”
the bishop sent to have him imprisoned in the city of Heraclea in Thrace. He was there for a period of two years without being allowed to see any of his acquaintances. As he was not given a change of clothes and was covered with innumerable lice, one of his disciples came and brought a change of clothes for him. But it was not again permitted for a change of clothes to be given to him, even through another.
Or: “But then it was not permitted for the change of clothes to be given to him, even by another.”
Afterwards, he grew ill to the point of death. He pronounced a curse in case he should be buried by the synodites or in case one of them should preside over or should offer a prayer for him. Thus, orthodox officers of the Romans, who happened to be there, brought him out to bury him with many candles and sweet spices. While they were enshrouding and processing his body like a great martyr, they condemned and reviled the persecution of such people. Then some of the faithful in the city determined to go retrieve the body of the holy one.

head.title: Chapter 10: On those things that happened in the monasteries of men and women through the savage violence of the persecution.

div.p: As for the monasteries of men and women which were in the imperial city, in all its suburbs and its districts, some of which had a copious number of inhabitants, especially those of women which at the beginning were driven out of Antioch, Isauria, Cilicia, Cappadocia, and the rest of the regions, having been assembled by the deceased empress Theodora, with some of the convents exceeding three hundred sisters in number – the torrent and squall of persecution also suddenly came upon them and the tempestuous cloud and turbulent fog of persecution engulfed them, that is, of the clergy together with the secular functionaries, namely, the komentaresioi and the taxeotai accompanying the domestikoi of the urban prefect , who were sent against them. With savage violence they encircled the monasteries. As a pack of wolves that enters and falls upon a fold of lambs, so did they spring up and enter each of the convents ravenously, against the young sheep of Christ. While the clergy were carrying their Eucharistic offerings, they dragged them along and violently compelled them to come receive communion from them. But all of them fled like sparrows before hawks and took shelter in nooks and crannies, wailing and saying: “We are not able to receive communion from the synod of Chalcedon which divides Christ, our God, into two natures after the union, and teaches a quaternity in place of the Holy Trinity.” In this way, through their intimidation and compulsion, they were dragged along to receive communion with their hands above their heads. While uttering laments, they were yanked along by their hands, shouting out wailing noises and sounds of weeping, with a raised voice, while trying to flee. Thus, by force the Eucharistic offering was set in their mouths, while they were uttering many things in wailing noises, with some of them casting themselves on their faces to the ground, condemning everyone who demanded that they receive communion by force. They subjugated some of them in this way. But everyone who resisted them and did not obey, they separated, dragged along, and expelled from their convents, and she was then handed over to the Roman taxeotai. As they were compelled and tugged along by them, they were led to the city and scattered there in different houses and places of confinement, in which, according to what was being said, such things were carried out by many as though by lustful stallions. As for our part, these will be passed over in silence, as the verdict on them is reserved for the righteous judge who will pass judgment on them and will demand vengeance for them. Thus, in this savage and barbarous manner such things happened in all the convents of men along with those of women.

head.title: Chapter 11: On John, the bishop of the city, and on those things that were carried out at his wicked instigation.

div.p: Now, the one who instigated, fostered, and carried out all these wicked things was John who, as has been said, was the head of the church of the city , the very one who out of the abundant bile of his wickedness came to make accusations before and to inflame the emperor in anger against the whole side of the faithful and departed after receiving free rein in regard to them. Through his partisans, he poured out the whole venom of his wickedness on all the ranks of the faithful in every place. He did this not only in his city and his diocese, but he also wrote to other regions, causing trouble and stirring up conflicts, persecutions, and wickedness. From that time on he personally went out and toured all the monasteries – as well as the houses – of men and of women, compelling and constraining them to come commune with him. But in his cruel anger he mercilessly gave an order to send and confine in monasteries all those who resisted him, one after another, whether men or women, whether monks, clergy, or cloistered women. He passed harsh death sentences against them. He managed to deceive, stir up, and force even the victorious emperors to yield to him and tour the monasteries one after another. First, he personally went with his clergy and held assemblies and performed consecrations in the monasteries, proclaiming the divisive synod in them and affixing images of himself in them. He appointed clergy to make the Eucharistic offering in them every Sunday and every feast and commemoration. Then, on the following day, the emperor personally toured the monasteries. The day after that, the empress did the same. They gave gifts to and established stipends for those who had obeyed or would obey them. But they sent those who resisted into banishment and oppressive places of confinement or handed them over to torments in the praetoria without mercy. They were given over to bitter and harsh floggings and tribulations in a rage and with bitter wrath.

head.title: Chapter 12: On the priesthood of the orthodox whom John thoughtlessly, lawlessly, and uncanonically deposed, reordaining them to the priesthood of the synodites, that is, of the dyophysites.

div.p: Then, when that bishop was filled with a spirit of zealous opposition and led by violence and rage, as though blind in the eyes, so also did he become blind through the hostile passion within him. He became as audacious as a drunkard. With all manner of savage tyranny and compulsion, he had managed to subjugate many priests of the orthodox and to lead them by force to commune with him. After they had received communion, they were admitted by him into their priestly order, with the presbyters being admitted as presbyters by him, receiving communion while fully vested alongside his presbyters, and sitting with them in a row in the hierateion . In the same way the deacons also carried out the entire task of the diaconate along with his own deacons, not only once or twice, but up to thirty-six times in all the festal synaxeis . And so they presided alongside him in their priestly vestments and carried out the entire task of the priesthood. Then, after this, a savage thought occurred to him, as though he were a young boy pompously elated in his pride and drunk on power and conceit. He issued an order, saying: “All of those who were opposed to us who have now acquiesced to us are ordered to be deposed from their original priestly rank and to become priests again through us.” Thus, after a time, he deposed all of them after they had officiated as a priest alongside him and in his presence thirty-six times on the basis of their former priestly rank which was from the orthodox. He reordained all those who had communed with him, even as they, agitated and troubled, condemned and reviled him and his lawless priestly ordination. He appointed some of them as the clergy of the same church, while many – even some of his partisans – condemned the transgression of the law and that which had happened wickedly and savagely through him against the canonical order. But, since the wicked deed that he had personally done and carried out was not enough for him, he also wrote to other regions and exhorted the bishops to behave like and as him, conspiring and scheming that he might not be condemned and censured alone because of the things which had come to pass through him against the law and the order but so that others might be found to be counted along with him as under condemnation and censure.

head.title: Chapter 13: On the nocturnal vision that like a revelation came to a certain blessed and excellent person concerning the things that immediately came to pass in the open.

div.p: Now, a certain blessed and excellent person had previously received a revelation in a nocturnal vision about those things which immediately, without delay in fact happened in the church of God. This is what he saw: There was a single large mountain which was very broad. On the southern side of this mountain, there was a large grove of many churches, which had been built in a row, one after another, over a very broad area. They were densely populated, beautiful, fair, and numerous. He saw John, bishop of the imperial city, suddenly come along with his clergy and a multitude of people alongside him. John ran violently against them, and he began uprooting and demolishing all the churches. He tore out and cast down all of their altars until he had obliterated the churches . Almost immediately after this vision, a few days later, this very thing was in fact fulfilled, as he came out, uprooted, and cast down all the densely populated communities of the churches of the faithful which were in the city and all its suburbs. Just as the revelation and the vision that had previously been revealed, so did it transpire in the open immediately.

head.title: Chapter 14: On Paul, a bishop from Asia.

div.p:
Now, as for this Paul, whom we mentioned above a little earlier, who was from the region of Caria and had been ordained and named the bishop of Aphrodisias, the metropolis of Caria , a modest and simple old man – while this one was quietly dwelling in his monastery, as we previously related, the patriarch John sent to have him apprehended and led away in fetters and to be imprisoned in his episcopal palace . While constraining him bitterly, he subjected him to communing with him. Then he sent him out to the same region. He wrote and ordered someone in the same city
Namely, the Chalcedonian metropolitan of Aphrodisias.
to depose him from his episcopal rank and to reordain him bishop in the city of Antioch in Caria . This in fact came to fruition. They deposed him, and, as though they thought they had defrocked him of his priestly rank, they then ordained him again as if he were a lay person. In this way, derision and mockery came from them and even from his own people, as his clerics called him “the twice-dipped.”

head.title: Chapter 15: On the bishop Elisha.

div.p: John then sent to have the old man Elisha, the bishop, led from the monastery called Beth Dios in which he was imprisoned so that he might be imprisoned in his episcopal palace . Out of great tribulation, Elisha was compelled to yield to taking communion with him. At that time, Elisha was scheming if it might in some way be possible to escape his hands. But John wanted to send him to Sardis, the metropolis of Lydia . Since John wanted to depose him from his episcopal rank and then to reordain him, Elisha resisted him, saying: “As for me, even though I am not worthy, I have been ordained a bishop by the orthodox. But as for you, you are not going to reordain me in any way. But if you still deem it orderly to depose and reordain me, then depose me first of the baptism I received and then do it a second time.” John said to him cunningly: “I am only placing the cloak on you.” But Elisha was not at all persuaded to yield to him, nor did he accept his words. John became angry at him and sent to have him imprisoned in another monastery called Beth Abraham. A harsh punishment was imposed on Elisha. He was in tribulation there for a long time and thus fell severely ill. He asked to go to a thermal bath, and he was released to do so with some guards.

head.title: Chapter 16: On the bishop Stephen whom John also wanted to depose and then reordain.

div.p:
So, when the mighty John was conceitedly lifted up so as to violate and transgress all the ecclesiastical ordinances, he sent to the island called Plateia , where the bishop Stephen had been banished by him, one of those who had a little earlier firmly rebuked him for abolishing the ordination. He sent clergy and excubitores against him and ordered them to have him tormented with lead balls until blood gushed forth, unless he should be persuaded to commune with them. Thus he was tormented with lead balls by twelve excubitores until he fell down between them, became silent, and was just about dead. Upon seeing that he had fallen down, become silent, and was approaching death, they ran and brought four pitchers of water and emptied them on him. Then, after a long period of time, life returned to him and it was as though he came back to life from the dead. In this way, he yielded to communing with them out of compulsion, especially since it became known that some of the faithful who were sending him what he needed had been both apprehended and imprisoned because of him and his opponents were preparing to fall upon them and despoil them. In this way, they took him and entered the city; many things were set in motion among them; and he communed with them out of compulsion. Afterwards, John demanded of him that he depose him and then reordain him as bishop for the island of Cyprus . But Stephen, striving against him and resisting him, created a commotion and began shouting “O bia!
The Syriac word is a transliteration of the Greek exclamation ὦ βία.
If you want to depose me from the priesthood of the orthodox and reordain me, also depose me of baptism first. First rebaptize me, then depose me from the priesthood, and then reordain me as a priest! For as the Lord God lives, unless you have rebaptized me, I will not ever accept it!” Thus, when there was a great commotion in the church between them and many had gathered,
Stephen promptly ran and went to the emperor , agitating him as well and shouting “O bia!”
The Syriac word is a transliteration of the Greek exclamation ὦ βία.
while saying: “Christianity has been lost! The ordinances of the Christian church have been corrupted! All the ordinances and canons of the church of God have been marred, violated, and destroyed! What lawless evil is this that the priesthood of the orthodox Christians is being stripped from those who now possess it and in its place a second one is being remade? Look, twenty years ago I, though unworthy, was canonically ordained as bishop by the orthodox at the order of Theodosius, patriarch of Alexandria . But now that I have given myself over and yielded to all of you, this one – committing a wicked act – wants to depose me from the priesthood of the orthodox, just as he has also done to many, and reordain me as one of his own. Let him reveal from which canons he learned this! Either he was not aware of and did not know the ecclesiastical canons, or although he knew them, he disregarded them and violated them in his conceit, pride, and deceptiveness. If the order was from you and he is doing these things with your knowledge, everyone will know! But you should know that he is seeking to cast the blame and censure of the canons on your your majesty, and he is conspiring to count you with him in the transgression of the ecclesiastical laws. If he with your knowledge and at your order is abolishing the priesthood and remaking a second one, then order him to abolish the baptism as well, to rebaptize, and then to reordain the priests. For the nineteenth canon of the 318 fathers, concerning the corrupt heresy of the followers of Paul of Samosata and others like him, orders them to be rebaptized and then those who are worthy among them to become priests again – this because of the wickedness of their heresy. So now, let this one first reveal for what reason he came to this and became so puffed up in his conceit as to depose and reordain us!” As the emperor heard these things and realized that Stephen was rightfully censuring and contending against him canonically, he was startled as though from a deep sleep. He condemned and even despised this deed, saying: “It is truly wicked, lawless, and against the whole ecclesiastical order for the priesthood to be abolished and be made again. It is detestable and quite foreign to all the ordinances of the church.” He then ordered that this not take place in the church of God in any way and immediately made a typos pragmatikos that it was not permitted for anyone to venture to abolish the priesthood again, unless it should happen in regard to heresies concerning which the canons give an order. If bishops happen to be doing this, their deposition would take place and they would be cast into banishment. When this had taken place and upon learning that a severe order had been issued, John and his advisors schemed to make a bribe so that the typos, which concerned this matter, might be taken from their midst and not be seen again. There was great enmity between John and Stephen because of this for all of their days.

head.title: Chapter 17: On the bishops being sent for and summoned from the monasteries and places where they were imprisoned.

div.p:
Hence, with a cunning thought and misleading duplicity – as the event itself imparted at the end –
John along with his advisors sent for and had the bishops brought whom he had cast into banishment in monasteries and other places, misleading them about having them brought to speak with them about the union. After first sending for and taking Paul, the patriarch, from the monastery of the Akoimetoi , and imprisoning him in his episcopal palace, he brought and imprisoned the rest of them, one after another with him: that is, Paul, John, Stephen, and Elisha . After first plotting and deliberating, they sent them a sheet on which something was written about the union that took place between Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch , having sent them a message in their place of confinement: “According to this union that took place between Cyril and John, you are also compelled to enter into a union with us.” Upon receiving these things, they consulted with one another and mocked their mischievousness. They sent them a message: “You have deliberated well. So, as for us, that which we have done and carried out is according to what Cyril did. We condemned, expelled, and cast it out from the church of God, that is, the synod of Chalcedon, just as Cyril did the evil Nestorius. Concerning the remaining things that happened between us,
Literally: “between you and us.”
we do not quarrel with you. Rather, we are united with you without any hindrance. But if it does not please you that that which Cyril did is also executed by us, how and in what way are you then conspiring to demand from us the union between him and John that came at the end, as though the earlier union of Cyril had not come to pass.”

head.title: Chapter 18: On the reprimand and censure that John received from the bishops that he had gathered and imprisoned in regard to the deposition and reinstatement of the ordination which was lawless and against all regulations and ordinances of the church of God.

div.p:
After these things, John was censured by the bishops who were imprisoned by John in his episcopal palace. They entered into a debate with him everyday concerning the faith, the corruption of the synod of Chalcedon, and the things carried out by it. They would begin by firmly censuring him, debating him, and rebuking him powerfully, saying to him as though he were being interrogated: “Reveal to us, O great one
Or: “teacher.”
and wielder of the ecclesiastical primacy, from which canon and ecclesiastical ordinance you have learned and received to abolish the priesthood of the orthodox bishops and the rest of the priests who are even senior to your father in the days of their priesthood so that you might abolish it and reordain them into the priesthood of the dyophysites who proclaim and teach a quaternity instead of the hidden and holy Trinity? In which ecclesiastical ordinance did you find this and follow it so that you might abolish the priesthood of the true orthodox and reordain them in its place with another priesthood of the synodites? On what occasion, in relation to which censure, in the case of which heresies, or according to what that is ordered in the canons, have you received this and did you depose those who are censuring you and fleeing from communing with you, because of the heresy of the dyophysites and because of the blasphemies of the synod and the Tome of Leo which proclaim and teach a quaternity in place of the holy Trinity? As for you, you cannot in any way censure and condemn them for heresy and then carry out the deposition of their priesthood. If then you do have something against those whom you lawlessly deposed from the priesthood, make it known to us now, and we will desist from the judgment and the censure in regard to these things! For if you granted yourself leave, against the ordinances and canons of the church, to make use of these measures and depose the true priests and reordain them, then this would also have been appropriate: that you also would have abolished their baptism and rebaptized them according to the force of the canonical commands. Indeed, the nineteenth
The manuscript has “sixteenth.”
canon of the 318 about the condemned heresy of the followers of Paul of Samosata ordered first that they be rebaptized and then that those who seem worthy become priests again. So, as for you, if you are now thinking to yourself that you have received from heresy those whom you led by force as captives and reordained as priests by yourself, how do you carry out the one when you have ignored the former?” As for John, he listened to all these things in silence and recognized that they were condemnable, since he did not have any way of putting up a defense concerning them. Eventually, he gave an answer, saying: “Since I, having listened to all these things from you silently, see that you are troubled and offended by the matter of the ordination, if the matter concerning the ordination is rectified and removed, will this suffice for you and will you enter into a union with us?” They said to him: “What rectification can you make, then, after all this damage and harm and the lawless deed that you have done? For, it was not only here that you carried this out and did not hinder the violence of your rage and hatred, but you also extended this transgression of the law to other regions along with the opposition to all ordinances and all ecclesiastical canons which either since they were not known to you, you broke and rejected, violated and transgressed, or granting yourself leeway in regard to them, you treated them with contempt and disparaged them, slipping into this whole transgression of the law and rejecting it. Let it not escape you that at whatever time it is that it
Or: “he.” That is, “time” or “God” summons, as suggested by Brooks.
summons, either during your life or, should it happen, after your death, all of these things will need to come to an orderly inquiry and a canonical investigation, so long as the world remains and the church of God exists! Now, as for the other thing you did at the end: it is fit for wonder, a fable, and a perplexing
Or: “astonishing.”
tale, whether you carried this out in the violence of rage and hatred, in the conceit of power, or for whatever reason. Now, you judge in your mind and consider whether you carried this out rightfully and with a rigorous examination, after you fell upon those in captivity whom you had rapaciously taken captive, pressing and subjugating them to commune with you. They carried out with you thirty-six Eucharistic anaphoras and the liturgies through the entire feast of unleavened bread
That is, Easter
and afterwards, as you accepted their original priestly rank and had them sit as priests in the hierateion with your priests for all the days with a sanctification, which have been mentioned. You also accepted the deacons in their diaconal task along with the rest of the deacons and established them on their step. Then, finally, after all these things, you in a certain foreign impudence acquiesced without consideration to abolishing their former priestly rank. That which we have now left for you to consider in your judgment and the examination in your mind is: Why depose them from the priesthood and reordain them? If it then seemed good to you so to act against the order and ecclesiastical canons before you had accepted them alongside you during the service of the Eucharistic anaphora, you could have done so. But not after all the liturgies in which they engaged and communed alongside you in their original priestly rank, can you go back, depose them and reordain them!” After many such things had been said by both sides – which we omit because of their copiousness and the length of the story about them – the censured one recognized in himself that he was approaching blame and was not free from censure, if he examined what he had done and especially what had been done by him at the end: deposing them after having accepted them in the service and then going back and deposing them. Because of these things, it was apparent through his silence that he found himself condemnable, as he conducted himself in a disorderly fashion through his base and empty language against them.

head.title: Chapter 19: On the edict that the emperor, the victorious Justin, crafted.

div.p:
When the partisans of the synod saw that some of those who had been seduced had then come over to them, the victorious emperor Justin came to craft an edict thinking he would create a union through it. After having it diligently drafted, he immediately sent it to the bishops who were imprisoned in the episcopal palace through a certain sophist Zacharias, the archiatrus palatii,
That is, archiatrus sacri palatii.
who was, in regard to his family, from Shura of the Romans and, as it was thought, formerly orthodox. This one, sent by the emperor to them with a draft of the edict that they arranged to have drafted, then said to them: “The merciful emperor has sent you this edict which he arranged to have drafted for your sake so that through it you and all your followers might enter into a union. He has granted you leave and also ordered this: when you have read it, you should rectify in it everything that you see as lacking in it and in need of correction. If something is lacking that is fit for a correct confession, which you want to have retained, you should add it to the edict without hesitation.” When the bishops received this order, they took the draft, read it, and saw that it was incomplete. Even if there was something in it opposed to the synod of Chalcedon, there was also something found in the draft from the council and in favor of it. Since they had been ordered, they made a set of chapters, that is, amendments. If their opponents had permitted these to be inserted into the edict, it would have undoubtedly created a union. The one who had brought the draft took the amendments, presenting and showing them to his confidant and advisor John and all the rest of those in their council, who when they heard the amendments were alarmed and terrified, as great fear fell upon them that if the amendments were retained, they would tear out the whole heresy of the dyophysites at its roots. Even more than the semi-Nestorians, the proper Nestorians were also alarmed and troubled, and they stirred up trouble and caused an uproar in the whole church, by running around and troubling the clergy and the people, saying: “If these additions are kept, the whole church is going to be disrupted and devastated.” Thus, they eventually gathered together their whole faction and went to the emperor to petition him that the amendments not be inserted into his edict, since they also troubled those belonging to the palace many of whom were not orthodox in regard to Christianity and especially the quaestor who in addition to being a pagan was a Samaritan, whose name was Anastasius of Palestine . When they came before the emperor and these amendments were read to him, he was pleased with them, praised them very much, and ordered that they be inserted into the edict and written on clean copy. But then all those who had come, the clergy, the secular functionaries, and the rest of the senators, opposed him, saying: “If you, my lord, insert the chapters into your edict and they enter the church, it will immediately be overturned and devastated. Although you want to bring in and retain a few people, masses are going to leave the church.” With some of them agitated at him, he was enraged and turned his face to them, saying: “These chapters are correct. But I know that all of you are Nestorians and sick in your mind, and you do not rejoice in the correct
Or: “orthodox.”
faith. Unless you keep silent, I am going to release and send the bishops out, dispatching them against you like wolves falling upon you and tearing you in bits and you will not be able to stand before them.” Then he ordered the quaestor, saying: “Unless you immediately bring me twenty copies of the edict before the evening, which have these amendments in them, I am going to remove your head!” Thus, the whole faction of the Nestorians and semi-Nestorians was alarmed, and they were buzzing about as wasps driven out by smoke. In this way, they were able to implore the emperor and terrify him about troubling the church. When they had pestered him enough, he submitted to them and left the matter according to their will. Only allowing a certain few amendments to be inserted, they heretically inserted on their own at the end of the edict, “Let the customs of the church be retained for the church,” so that, in regard to the synod, they might proclaim it as their custom. They conspired together and deviously inserted this so that there might not be a union and the wheel turn against them as Nestorians.

head.title: Chapter 20: After the edict had been written on twenty clean copies its first subscriber sent it to those who were imprisoned.

div.p: After, as the emperor had ordered, the edict was written on clean copy, he immediately subscribed it and sent it to the bishops who were in the episcopal palace, sending a message to them: “Now we have created the conditions for a union, exactly as you want. We have sent you the edict, and henceforth you have no reason to resist entering into the union with us. For I even made this edict on your behalf.” But when the bishops read the edict and saw that the amendments they had made were not in it but only bits of them had been inserted according to the will of those who tore it up, they disregarded it because their opponents had marred and muddled it according to their will but, out of fear of the emperor, did not venture to remove those things opposed to the dyophysites from it. They rather schemed insofar as they were able to insert their own views into it, with the edict appearing to oppose the synod and also to fight on its behalf, as some things from the council and from its adherents appeared in it. The bishops gave an answer to those who brought it, saying: “If then the cause of offense and disturbance of the whole church, that is, the synod of Chalcedon, is removed from the midst, the church will also not need this. But if the synod is proclaimed in the church, not even from a thousand edicts like this one, affixed in all places and corners, will a union arise. But a schism will persist in its place. For this edict seems to be against the synod and to fight on its behalf, and both can be recognized in it by those who understand.”

head.title: Chapter 21: On John testifying against the bishops, saying: “See now, you are the ones obstructing the union in the church!”

div.p: John testified every day against the bishops alongside those sent to them in the name of the emperor , saying: “See now, you are the ones obstructing and preventing the union in the church of God. The union already came about fifty years ago, but now you are driving it out, hindering it, and holding it back, as you do not want to enter into a union.” But the bishops replied to them, saying: “How are we the ones obstructing the union to which you draw near not with any of your fingers but rather with exterior words and deviousness so that you might seem and be perceived by people to be ‘the ones who truly care about the union,’ even as you conspire to cast the entire blame from everyone on us? What then is the union that you are taking care to create? Or in what way are you demanding that we enter into a union with you, when you are the ones holding onto the synod which has uprooted and caused trouble for the whole church of God, as you proclaim and cherish it? If then you truly are taking care to create the union as you say, remove the stumbling block and cause of the offense from the clear path of the faith and bring it away from the church of God, so that in this way not only we ourselves but also all the faithful, rejoicing and being free from the offense, will enter into a union with you” – along with many other such things which we would not be able to relate in full even on a thick stack of sheets, we have left out and omitted those things which were said between them concerning these matters every day in the various exchanges of the debate because of the copiousness of the words and the thickness of the texts, lest they be thought an irritation by those who chance upon them.

head.title: Chapter 22: On the bishops being censured and blamed even by the leaders of the orthodox because of their obstinacy and inflexibility concerning the union.

div.p: Before the time of this persecution and in addition to the trials, torments, and imprisonments from their persecutors, the bishops had been harshly censured by the rest of the orthodox, who strove against them, saying: “Why do you keep on quarreling and being so obstinate and do not show prudence and flexibility so that there might be a union in the church of God? You rather keep on being so stiff-necked and quarreling with those in power without being concerned about the fact that you are going to lose us, our houses, our children, and our property. What does it concern you that we are going to lose our assets and become beggars?” Since they had previously been told such things in the harsh censures of the orthodox out of anxiousness regarding their wealth and in addition to them the synodites were testifying about them every day – “Now, you are obstructing the union from coming about” – they fell day and night into suffering and bitter crying as they sat crying and wailing with groans to each other. With wailing sounds, they were saying: “What will we do from this point forward? For now we are being censured by both sides. They are testifying against us and blaming us, and we, imprisoned in torment, are not even allowed to see any of our acquaintances. Our opponents especially say these things to the leaders and the noteworthy among us. They cunningly say and profess to everyone to be the ones who care about the union, and then with everyone thinking their words are true they cast the entire allegation of obstructing the union on us. Now, we have found ourselves in the middle of the two sides. We know that the truth is not in them so that we might give ourselves over and trust the cunning promises and duplicitous oaths of our opponents, and we know that they are not at all worthy so that the union might be effected through them. Since we keep resisting them, we are going to be condemned by both sides through the generations of the world as those who obstructed and held back the union in the church of God, while they deem as true their cunning promises and their duplicitous words as though they were prepared to create a union but we kept the union from the church.” Along with the many other such things that were said between them with grief-stricken tears over many days to whose truth we testify before God, as those who were nearby and eyewitnesses of all of them.

head.title: Chapter 23: On the debate and the torment of the bishops and their retinue.

div.p:
For thirty-three days they ardently engaged in a debate with each other – the partisans of the synod who wielded power and the opponents of the synod who were being imprisoned and bitterly tormented by them. Everytime the opponents of the synod would be sent for, be released, and come out of the place of confinement, they would come to the patriarchal sekreton , sit down, and debate as much as those in power wanted. Whenever the partisans of the synod had either been defeated by them or when it pleased the partisans of the synod, the bishops would go out alongside their guards, going back into and being imprisoned within the three guard posts: that of the bishop’s attendants, that of the excubitores, and an external one of the doorkeepers. The retinue of each of the bishops from everywhere – not only clerics, monks, and the remaining free persons with them, but also all their servants – were imprisoned in dark and bitter places of confinement all over the episcopal palace . All of them were under guard, and not only that, but they were stripped down – as though by robbers – by the bishop’s attendants and the dekanoi . If they had any clothes on, they were stripped down even to a rag that they could not use. If a shoe, belt, strap,
Literally: “belts, straps.”
or money was found on any of them or anything else whatsoever, they would be stripped down. Everything that was found on them was removed, and they were cast on the ground being stripped down and tormented, even in regard to their provisions. Those who would see them or offer them something, either to them or to the bishops, would not be allowed to do so in any way at all. Instead of the promise of the union, they and all those with them endured and underwent this wickedness, this adversity, and these trials, especially because they were openly rebuking those torturing them.

head.title: Chapter 24: On the final debate and the cunning and duplicitous oaths.

div.p:
So, after all these things, the bishops were again finally sent out of and led away from their place of confinement. Then the leader of the session, John, and even more than him those sent by the emperor and doing so with his authority, set themselves harshly and shamelessly against the bishops , saying: “How long are you going to cast away
A marginal note suggests reading this word as “neglect.”
and obstruct the union in the church of God which our lord the emperor and we are taking care to create? Day by day you hold back and cast away this good thing, yet you do not openly expose to everyone that you yourselves are the ones who are obstructing it and no one else. Now then, in short: either enter into the union or reveal yourselves as the ones causing trouble for, assailing, and obstructing the union in the church!” But the bishops, who were mourning and suffering, said: “If these matters are adjudicated justly and with honest intentions, it is not we who are holding back the union but you. For even though your innermost heart is filled with the venom of opposition and the divisiveness of Chalcedon, you want to reveal us as the ones holding it back, even though you have neither put forth nor showed in any way in yourselves even the shadow of a union. Rather, you are cunningly demanding that we enter into a union with the duplicity of Chalcedon. You are not taking care to create a true union in the church in a just and correct manner! Now, just as we have said from the beginning: if you truly want a union and are not intent on carrying out a cunning act of deceit and a wicked plot, first remove the cause of the divisiveness from the midst and then the union will rise in its place. We are not the ones whom you should cunningly accuse of this! But now, since this has not at all occurred to you,
Literally: “to your mind.”
why are you swindling and slandering us everyday? And increasing our pain with torments and the tribulation of imprisonment along with the rest of the irreverent tribulations that we endure from you everyday? And torturing us every day with your words and beating our sores with thorns? And showing no fear of God for you see that our lives are already coming to an end? They are passing away and reaching their completion through the tribulations by which we are surrounded on all sides. If then you are set on creating a union in accordance with your external words, expel the synod of Chalcedon which has caused trouble, which has divided, and which has caused a schism in the church, as you also cannot deny, and in this way a union without division will arise in the whole church of God.” Then John and those with him said: “It is this very thing – that the synod be expelled as you are saying – which you are obstructing! For if you enter into a union, the synod will immediately be expelled and there will be complete unity.” The bishops said: “It seems to us that you are taking care to make us enter into a union with your synod. It should not escape you or anyone else that until the breath of the nostrils ceases in each of us, the condemnation of the synod and the Tome of Leo which as one divide our Lord, our God, and our savior Jesus Christ into two natures after the union will not cease from our mouths.” John and those with him said: “Just as we have said to you many times, we and our lords, the emperors are giving you our word and making oaths as though before God that the synod will immediately be removed when you enter into a union and that which goes out of our lips will not be changed.” The bishops said: “If you really are prepared to do this in accordance with your oaths, why then do you not do it immediately so that not only we but also everyone might enter into a union without an offense? But you rather want to deceive us cunningly, have us enter into a union with the heresy of the dyophysites, and afterwards mock us again. It should be known to you that you are mocking and deceiving yourselves. For we know that your will and your thoughts are not of the union, since you do not even seem to be speaking the truth even if in words you are prepared to create it, so that it might be known to everyone that we have obstructed the union with the result that we might be condemned, by everyone just as by you, as those who are obstructing it. We are even sacrificing ourselves for the union as we, trusting in your words and your promises that the synod will from this point forward be condemned and expelled, are going to commune with you, be it once or, should it happen, twice, while unceasingly having the condemnation of the synod on our mouth. But we will never have any part or share in communion with you for a third time before the synod has been condemned and expelled. We know that you are not upholding the truth in your words! In order that it might become clear and be known to everyone that you are not prepared to create the union but rather to think that you will deceive us and everyone, we are now going to hand ourselves over to forming a union with you, be it up to two times.” Such things and many others which are innumerable were spoken and testified to between them. The bishops concluded in this way, saying: “As for us, due to the slanders against us from the synodites, we are now handing ourselves over so that it might be known that we are not obstructing the union.” For everyone from both sides reviled them, saying: “The emperors and patriarch are taking care to, intend to, and are preparing to create a union. But those who are imprisoned are hindering and holding them back.” Wherefore, because of these things, they handed themselves over with great suffering. While condemning the synod of Chalcedon with a raised voice, they acquiesced to commune with them two times as they had promised and to which they had conceded, after they had been boldly demanding for all thirty-three days from the emperor and the patriarch the condemnation and the expulsion of the synod by those who had pronounced many oaths. But then the Chalcedonians began to go back on their words and were giving the bishops various excuses, saying: “We are going to write to the pope in Rome and then, if he accepts it, we are going to expel the synod. For we cannot cause a schism with Rome on your behalf.” Then the bishops replied to them, saying: “Now it is also the time for us to recount
the word of the prophet Jonah: Is this not what I said when I was in my land?
” In this way, it became known and clear to everyone that it was not those who were imprisoned and enduring bitter tribulations who were the ones obstructing the union, who had in fact handed themselves over for its sake. Rather those who were in power and who were slandering them violated their promises and their oaths to them, as they wanted to have the entire scandal pinned on the bishops not only by their contemporaries but also by generations to come, as if the bishops had prevented and obstructed the union in the church of God and had not sacrificed themselves on its behalf even though the bishops suspected those in power of deceit.

head.title:
Chapter 25: On the mourning and brokenness of spirit that came upon the bishops because they had yielded and entered into a union through taking part in communion
Literally: “communions.”
with John and his fellow dyophysites.

div.p:
Who is able to write about and recount the adversities, mourning, lamentation, and brokenheartedness that came upon those who had succumbed to communing with the ones who had tricked them and entered into a union with them without being united at all? How from that point on they were perishing and coming to an end, with their eyes swelling up and growing dark from crying and lamenting night and day to each other,
the example of a woman mourning for the husband of her youth being small in comparison to their mourning?
How from that point on they also cut themselves off from regular food and would wait without provisions and without consolation, having no pause or respite from tears, while everyone with his face covered would turn away from the others, as they sat from that time on and lamented with bitter sounds of groaning, one to another, especially in regard to themselves, who after all the struggles, imprisonments, and tribulations, had been seized, dragged down and succumbed in this way to their duplicitous cunning, while the union remained far off and had not materialized as promised? How from that point on those who had tricked them, having seen that they had already perished and been ruined, would say to them: “To what end are you killing your soul along with your body in this way as if you were making a sacrifice to idols? Why have you imposed this upon yourselves and have you persisted in all this crying and lamentation, continually choking yourselves with crying? Take food and be consoled! Live and do not die!” – along with the many other things said to them by their opponents,
as they remained inconsolable? Thus they sat all the more lamenting and crying and were embittered for many days even after they had been released and gone forth from the place of confinement. They then returned and stood their ground valiantly, as the bishops all the more openly rebuked the Chalcedonians and strove against them from that time forward, deriding them as liars and tricksters. They were thus handed back over to their prison cells and to torments harsher than the former ones, and each one of them was cast into different places of banishment in a bitter wrath.

head.title: Chapter 26: On the emperor, having learned about these things, sending for and having them led to the palace and consoling them.

div.p: When the emperors learned about all the severe mourning and the bitter suffering, they sent for and had the bishops led to the palace. They consoled them, saying: “Why has such mourning become necessary for you so that you have become so weak that it is as though you are dead? Be encouraged and consoled, for we hope in God that we can satisfy you and enter into a full union with you. Do not be distressed!” But since they were censuring the emperor at length about the things discussed between them which had been violated and had not come to pass, he said to them: “Since we are preparing to go to the thermal bath, wait for me for twenty or thirty days and know that we will immediately come back for your sake. We are ready to discuss the matter and to satisfy you. The whole church will be one, and we will all rejoice!” And so they were dismissed by him, having expressed their many rebukes in his presence.

head.title: Chapter 27: On that which happened after the emperor returned from the thermal bath, the register being sent out, and so forth.

div.p:
After thirty days the emperor returned from the thermal bath as he had promised. As he had not yet entered the city, but was rather on the opposite bank in Chalcedon, the patriarch
John led his advisors and went out to him, as later became apparent, to bring additional accusations against those who had been imprisoned, saying: “They have cut themselves off from us and have entirely turned away from the church.” Then they deliberated how to take hold of their will and also deceive them. They made a register of all the large cities and sent to them through the
quaestor
who brought a message to them: “Since we are concerned about satisfying you, we care about your honor, and we want to offer you our steadfast support, we have now sent you this register of all the noteworthy cities. We did not designate by ourselves over which city each of you should be, but we left this to your will so that each of you might choose for yourself a pleasing city.” But when the illustrious
quaestor took the register and entered the city, he sent for and summoned the bishops so that he might give the register to them, while saying all these things along with many other things to them as one sent in the name of the emperor. But they did not look at the register, nor did they want to receive it from him, saying: “As for us, we are not sacrificing ourselves so that we might be bishops over cities – tell the victorious emperor this! – but rather in the hope of the promises of the union – for though we are bishops, we are unworthy ones – unless the promises of the union are fulfilled
The translation “unless the promises of the union are fulfilled” reflects the main text in the manuscript. But a marginal note changes the phrase to read “but so that the promises of the union might be fulfilled.”
along with the oaths made to us over the length of many days. For the sake of these things and so that the slanders against us might not be confirmed – that we are obstructing the union – we gave ourselves over to communing with those who tricked us and thought they had deceived us but did not know that they had deceived themselves and not us. So now, if the synod is not expelled and a union does not come into being according to the promises and the oaths, far be it that we ever again have communion with those who commemorate the name of the synod! As they decidedly cut themselves off and did not want to receive it, the
quaestor grew angry and went to tell these things to the emperor. This one also became angry and was awakened in a great rage, intimidating them and making harsh threats, which he eventually carried out.

head.title: Chapter 28: On the emperor – having come to know these things and having become enraged – commanding all the high-ranking men to enter and the bishops to be judged in the episcopal palace .

div.p: Then, since the emperor was filled with rage, he sent for and summoned the patriarch of the city and met him harshly with harsh words of contempt, saying: “You have turned away the bishops who through much sweat and with difficulty promised to commune with us. But now you have turned them away from us!” Being troubled in this way, he ordered that the senators enter the episcopal palace so that the matters that had come between them would be adjudicated with the patriarch also being judged and, if they found him culpable, condemned as well.

head.title: Chapter 29: On them gathering, as they had been ordered, at the episcopal palace and the bishops being summoned to trial.

div.p:
For when, in accordance with the order, on the next day some senators along with the
quaestor entered the episcopal palace and the bishops were summoned to trial, and when they were required by order to remain in communion with the synodites, keeping silence and without making any further request, at that time they arose and fought powerfully and valiantly in the struggle: Without any fear they openly decried all the deceitful promises, all the oaths that had been violated, the truth that had been rejected and trampled upon, and other similar things. In addition to this, by anathematizing the entire heresy of the dyophysites openly to the senate, they employed great boldness. Just as they acted in a firm manner at the beginning, the middle, and then at the end, so did they again when they pronounced definitively and forever that they cut themselves off from and had put an end to communion with them. There were many other things that happened and were spoken there in the contest of the valiant fight, until the point that the anger of the senators and the patriarch grew hot against the bishops and they issued an order that they be expelled and dragged away from their presence, that they be separated from each other, and that they be cast into exile. This happened at once: they were expelled and were separated, and they did not see each other again. They were sent into exile, some to monasteries, some to islands of the sea, some to oppressive and bitter cells in hospices, since it had been decreed against them that they be imprisoned and that it be granted neither to one of their own nor to anyone else to see one of them. Many other things were decreed against them cruelly, harshly, and mercilessly in bitter wrath and with the fetters of iniquity, as though against murderers.

head.title: Chapter 30: On the defense of the author himself to those who succumb to a vain opinion of him.

div.p: Perhaps all the things that have been said and written will be deemed by those well-trained in lofty knowledge and swift-of-mind who judge such words sharply to be things said and written by the author superfluously and as the author’s exercise of the literary craft, as though it was in an embellished style that they were written by him. But if this is not the case, from where did he thus know how to relate and write in an orderly manner all the things that happened and were spoken between the two sides and to transmit all these things in writing? As it seems, he is orderly in his discourse with one side, shoots arrows against the other side, and only fights on behalf of the one side. But it should now be known to those who hold to such an opinion that the author of all these things was not an outsider to the struggle and was not far from the battle so that he only received this things by hearsay from a distance and from others and wrote and passed them down in his book. Rather he was one of those who was engaged in the struggle, and in the great battle along with the others he valiantly endured the sufferings even more and underwent the tribulations of persecutions and imprisonments. It was not only the few records that he transmitted in this book that were said and uttered in the debate with the emperor and to the patriarch, but a hundred times more. Omitting an abundance of these things due to the great length of the story and the density of the innumerable words, he only made use of a few of them. Although he was on the anti-dyophysite side which was arrayed against the dyophysites, as his discourse itself – that is, the order of the composition – shows, he maintains neutrality, without defaming and slandering the one side that was against him so as to establish his own side. Rather as at the beginning of this volume of his composition or as he previously said and promised, so was he an advocate for the truth and preserved the seal of truth for the two sides in regard to those things that were said, set down, and debated. Even if he only made use of a few of them, it sufficed to commit to writing less than one percent of the things that were spoken and took place.

head.title:
Chapter 31: On
Conon, the head of the heresy of the tritheists.

div.p:
At that time Conon was also apprehended, who was the head of the heresy of those who venture to enumerate the natures, substances, divinities, and gods in the holy and consubstantial Trinity. This one was likewise apprehended and imprisoned in the episcopal palace for a time with all the others. They demanded that he produce a libellus as a heretic. But he resisted this and did not do it. As soon as the victorious emperor learned this, he swore by the God-bearer, saying: “Even if he makes a petition and comes to receive communion in the church, unless he produces a libellus of repentance, I am not going to enter the church and receive communion in it.” Thus, because he was a heretic and a blasphemer, when
Photius the son of Belisarius’s wife came to the imperial city at that time, Conon was given to him. Photius took him along to Palestine and imprisoned him in the monastery called the “Nea.” He was there for a period of three years, and he was thus released and went to the region of Cilicia.

head.title: Chapter 32: On Photius and the things carried out by him.

div.p:
This Photius was the son of the wife of the patrikios Belisariuswhose name was Antonina. Then, when this one was in the army and had been sent out to war with Belisarius, he eventually for some reason went to cut off his hair and take on the monastic habit, although he did not at all draw near to a monastic life but took on the habit of a monk as
Or: “under.”
an assumed name. After a little time, when he was not able to subdue his savageness through the fear of God, this one ran to the emperor. Then he, wearing a monastic vestment in outward appearance, was sent to the regions of Syria because of the Samaritans who had rebelled. Since he wanted to please people and to anger God his maker and was also running after profane gains on various pretexts, he gave himself over to despoiling, plundering, and swindling people as well as to uprooting and destroying, as though by barbarian captors, all the regions great and small of the east, to the point that even the bishops and clerics of all the cities fled before him. Indeed, anyone whether in the city or in the country whom he knew to have a surplus of daily rations, he would apprehend, despoil, imprison, hang up, and torment and would impose on them a fine of pounds of gold – on anyone, whether he possessed it or not. Even if he would sell himself, his children, his house, and his possessions, there was no way for someone to change the word of his mouth. For when he apprehended anyone, he would say: “Give so many pounds, for the emperor is in need of gold for going out to war.” In this way he would gather hundreds of pounds and send them so that by this the authority would be given to him to do whatever he wanted to whomever he wanted and people would not rise against him. For he also made great demands even of bishops in the same way. If anyone challenged him, he would immediately without delay be raised up on a noose, either behind his head, or on his shoulders, or on one of his arms. So it is said that he imposed a fine of three hundred pounds on the bishop of Ascalon , and when he was wailing and cursing the fact that he did not have it, he gave an order and the bishop was hung by a noose. He left him hanging and went away, giving an order that should he hang there for three days, he would not come down from there until the hundreds of pounds came. Thus he did not come down from the noose until the three hundred pounds came. He did the same to many others until the earth shook before him along with the magistrates, governors, and the rest of the dignitaries. Since many were making appeals to the emperor, the emperor wrote to him: “It is a sin for you to send to us from what has been despoiled!” Then, he wrote to the emperor: “You, my lord, should not fear sin because of the gold that I am sending. This sin be on my own head.” He had a troop of monks with him who were suitable for these things, along with despotikoi, domestikoi, and spatharioi as well as an army of Roman soldiers. When he had completed a period of twelve years in this detestable manner of destruction, impudence, and savageness, contrary to the whole fear of God, his end came upon him and he was buried with a wicked end and a cursed memory. He was succeeded by another abbot of the monastery called the “Nea” in Jerusalem whose name was Abraham.

head.title: Chapter 33: On the sophists, scholastikoi, naukleroi, and the rest of those who in the middle of the persecution were summoned and went up from Alexandria.

div.p:
Then in the middle of the persecution the Chalcedonians wrote to and sent to
Literally: “it was written and sent to.”
Alexandria – sophists, scholastikoi, naukleroi, and many others went up – with on the one hand the hidden intention of demanding that they receive communion with the synod, but on the other hand with the outward appearance of having done this for the sake of the union, while having in fact both of these intentions in regard to them. When the Chalcedonians ultimately demanded that they receive communion, they resisted and debated valiantly for many days, about one year, without growing timid or yielding at all. But eventually some of them were released because those who were capable of doing this by force feared for the shipment of wheat which was sent from there to the imperial city . Some of them were apprehended for as long as three years, but nothing happened. In this way everyone was released.

head.title: Chapter 34: On all the chief clerics of the orthodox who were apprehended after these things and sent to the imperial city.

div.p:
After these things, it was written to the emperor
that the orthodox bishops had ordained not only one bishop but two as the successor of the blessed Theodosius . He became severely enraged and gave an order that all the chief clerics be apprehended and sent off, which is what came to pass. They were apprehended and went up until after the death of the patriarch John. Then came Eutychius, who had been patriarch before him. He arrived after John’s death and released the bishops after one year.

head.title: Chapter 35: About the blessed Egyptians who also had been summoned to the imperial city to make predictions about the things to come.

div.p: Now, many times envoys would be sent to Egypt. Many solitaries would then be summoned and come to the imperial city who were thought to know hidden things and to have an understanding of things to come in order that they might make known how many years the emperor and empress would live, what was going to happen during his reign, and other such things. When some of them were asked about these things, they would recuse themselves, confessing ignorance. They would rather speak about admonition, judgment, and righteousness: that should these come about, they would please God and be as offerings brought before him. In light of the fact that they were rejected for not acquiescing to give a flattering answer as had been asked of them, they were immediately dismissed and driven out of the city to go to their own region. But others, who were people-pleasers, acquiesced to weakness in this matter, which eventually would be rebuked. But they were held in honor, and were doted with pleasures and gifts of food for a long period of time. This happened many times.

head.title: Chapter 36: On the monasteries of men and women where a few, after being treated violently, assented to the synod but came back at the end.

div.p:
When all the monasteries mentioned above were being treated violently and lawlessly, a few of those in them took communion; the rest were cast into other monasteries. Clerics were appointed in all the districts so that they might bring Eucharistic offerings into each and every one of the monasteries and distribute them to those who assented. The name of the synod was recorded within the monasteries and proclaimed.
John took down the images of all the orthodox fathers and affixed images of himself everywhere. That which he did was avenged by God, for after his bitter and tortuous death and the arrival of Eutychius who had been bishop before him and who came again after his death, the images of him were torn out and those of Eutychius were affixed in the churches in their place. All the monasteries of women came back for the most part and became orthodox. But from the women, a few girls stayed behind and entered every day to receive communion with the synod. They took on the monastic habit and received communion from the clerics. But they cut themselves off openly and not a single one of them received communion, especially after the death of John.

head.title: Chapter 37: On John being asked before his death by the Christ-loving Caesarabout the orthodox.

div.p: Now, before John’s death, when he was drunk on the zeal of anger for the persecution of the Christians and thirsting like a wolf for the blood of lambs, he went to the peaceable and serene Caesar Tiberius , intent on also inflaming him with the zeal of anger like his own. After John had said many words to him against the believers, Tiberius asked him, saying: “I want you to answer me truthfully concerning those about whom I am asking you. Are the ones whom you are intent on having me persecute pagans?” Not being able to lie, he said to him: “They are not pagans.” He said to him: “If this is not the case, are they heretics?” He said to him: “They are not heretics, my lord.” He said to him: “Then, as you have testified about them, they are Christians.” Then, he said: “Christians, indeed. They are Christians.” He said to him: “If according to your testimony they are Christians, how are you intent on me becoming a persecutor of Christians as Diocletian and the other pagan emperors? Go sit in your church and be silent! Do not burden us with these matters.” In this way, his savage rage cooled until anger from heaven overtook him, as we have related above, and he passed away from life here. Then after this one, as we related above, when Eutychius had come and the clerics who were accustomed to despoiling and plundering had begun inflaming him, Eutychius also left and went to the conciliatory Tiberius Constantine, the Caesar, saying many things to him about the whole side of the believers. But he also replied to him, saying: “The wars of the barbarians which surround us on all sides are enough for us. We cannot again stir up another war against the Christians. Go sit and be silent! If then you are able to persuade with a word or an admonition… But, if not, leave them alone! Do not persecute them, and do not burden us who have been cast into the trials of wars on all sides!” In this way, Eutychius also receded and has been silent for a time.

head.title: Chapter 38: On the orthodox communities enduring in the end and persisting, even at the time John was alive.

div.p: So, as for all the communities of the orthodox which had been uprooted and closed by John of Sirmin , after God sent a chastisement of harsh anger from heaven upon him, they all grew emboldened and reopened. Even beforehand they had expanded and grown in fear, in silence, and gradually while he was alive – all the more so after he had been struck by an angry blow. From that time forward, his mind was engrossed in the deadly fire which had taken hold in his heart and burned in his innards. Accordingly, at the end his partisans and the servants of his wickedness who knew his will would enter and tell him: “Look! The enemies of the church and the synod have opened their gathering places, and they have spread out even more than before. They rejoice in your illness and are praying for your death. If you order us, we are going to torment them more than before and make them endure wicked things.” Then, enraged and with a raised voice, he strove against them, saying: “Go from me, murderers! Take satisfaction in my humiliation! For it was largely you who brought me to this humiliation and misery. These curses suffice, which have awakened the wrath of heaven and brought it upon me. Leave my presence! Let no one bring this matter before me again!” In this way, they departed from him dejected. But before his death, as we have said, and also after his death which soon came, they assembled in the gathering places of the faithful in full freedom of assembly, without fear.
