head.title: Chapter 39: On the monastery called Kathara in the region of Bithynia.

div.p:
Now, the monastery of Kathara was built by Narses when he was the chartularios of the emperor in the palace before he went to Rome, while he was preparing to go forth from the palace, to shave his head, and to dwell in it. He settled monks in it who had been driven out of Cappadocia , after buying not a few villages for it, building a wondrous church in it, establishing a hospice in it, and allocating it no small amount of revenue. In this way, he was sent to Rome. Then, through the help of God who accompanied him, he was celebrated for great victories in many wars. There, in these regions, he departed from the world and his bones came to and were placed in the monastery with the emperors
That is, Justin II and either Tiberius or Sophia.
carrying and placing him in it and consecrating him.

head.title: Chapter 40: On the synodite bishops who were in Alexandria.

p.seg: After completing some years in the episcopate of Alexandria John, who had been a patrikios in the imperial city and had been sent there, died.
A certain person named Eulogius who had been in charge of a hospice in Antioch was called in his place. He became pope there in the third year of the victorious Tiberius.
But of the faction of the Julianists it was Dorotheus who had been ordained and had resided there for many years.
And of the faction of the followers of Theodosius some time after his death it was first a certain person named Theodore, a monastic superior from Syria.

When the clerics and others learned this, they turned from him. Since they did not receive him against the canonical order, they brashly ordained another above him whose name was Peter. When this one had completed his time and ordained more than eighty bishops, he died.
They ordained some Syrian whose name was Damian, and they persisted in causing a schism.

head.title: Chapter 41: On the bishops who were in Antioch since Flavian and Severus.

p.seg: Now, in the great Antioch of Syria in the days of the emperor Anastasius was Flavian who was culpable of the heresy of the dyophysites. He was banished from his see when he had been there some years.

After him was the orthodox Severus, and he held the see for six years. But at the beginning of the reign of Justin I, the elder, he was expelled from his see. After some years being in the desert of Egypt, he died there.
When Antioch had been without a bishop for one year, Paul the Jew eventually went down.
He had been the paramonarios in the church of the blessed Euphemia in Chalcedon.
He took down the diptych on which the synod for the east was written.
After he held the position for about two years, it became known that he was a Nestorian. He was banished, and he was also expelled.
After him was Euphrasius, a Samaritan. In his seventh year, Antioch was overthrown by an earthquake, and he died in it.
Then after him was Ephrem of Amida, the son of Appianus, a persecutor more wicked than Paul and Euphrasius. He lived for some years, and he died.
After him was Domninius, a Roman […].
After him was Anastasios who had been the apokrisiarios of Alexandria. Justin II formulated various accusations against him and expelled him.
There was sent as his successor a certain abbot of Mount Sinai named Gregory.

Now, those opposed to the synod of Chalcedon, after a long period of time, first ordained in Severus’s place Sergius, a person from Tella in regard to his family. When he had completed about three years, he died in the imperial city where he had also been.

Then, after some time, there came in his place Paul, a certain Alexandrian, the synkellos of Theodosius of Alexandria, who at Theodosius’s order and with his knowledge became bishop in the wicked days.

A schism arose between him and the blessed Jacob, the church of the faithful was ruptured, and it was split into two divisions. With grueling conflicts and battles, the two sides fell upon each other.
Then, those opposed to Paul after the death of Jacob lawlessly appointed a certain person named Peter, who was from the city of Callinicum, as another patriarch in Antioch.
Such were the things that gradually unfolded up to the present time in which they are being written which is the year 892.
p.note: 892 of the Seleucid Era, which is 580/581 CE.

head.title: Chapter 42: On the bishops who were in Constantinople in the days of the reign of Justinian.

div.p:
In Constantinople
after Epiphanius, Anthimus was brought to the episcopate. He had previously been bishop of the city of Trapezus, and he was summoned to the imperial city. After he had been in the imperial city for a period of several years, when Severus of Antioch was summoned from Egypt by order of Justinian so that might they talk about the union and Anthimus learned through his debate with Severus about the maliciousness and perversity of the synod of Chalcedon and about the blasphemies of Leo in his Tome, he left the throne of the imperial city. He was expelled and entered into a union with Severus and Theodosius of Alexandria. Then after this one, a certain Menas from Alexandria who had been in charge of the hospice of Samson sat on the throne of the imperial city. When he had completed some years, he left the world and was succeeded by a certain young monk, the apokrisiarios of Amasia, whose name was Eutychius . When he had held the see for about twelve years, he was banished and expelled from his see. After him came John, a certain Syrian from the village of Sirmin in the region of the Antiochians. When he was bishop, he deposed Eutychius and Eutychius did the same to him. When John had completed twelve and a half years, he died. Then Eutychius was summoned, and he returned to his see.

p.title: Ended is the first volume of the stories of the church in which forty-two chapters are included.
